headline: Wheat Drops 14¢, Milk Rockets 15% Higher
subheadline: Corn and beans ease on dollar strength while dairy posts biggest single-day jump in months.
teaser: Grains soften on dollar strength, milk surges, rain coming Thursday.

one_number.why: Biggest dairy price pop in months signals tighter supply heading into spring flush—lock margins if you haven't.

field_call.label: PLAN AHEAD
field_call.detail: Dry through Wednesday across most of the Belt, then widespread rain Thursday knocks out burndown window through weekend.

sections.title: Grains & Oilseeds
bullets: Corn $4.45¼, down 2¾¢. Dec '26 at $4.69¼. The 24¢ carry is flat—new-crop still not cheap enough to make storage pencil.
Beans $11.61¾, down 8½¢. Meal at $312.50, oil at 62.68¢. Crush margins squeezed as meal lost more ground than oil.
Wheat $5.76¼, down 14½¢ on 2.45% drop. Black Sea competition heating up and specs bailed. Spring planting intentions coming later this month.
USDA crush report: 228 million bushels of beans processed in January, up from December but below trade expectations.

sections.title: Livestock & Dairy
bullets: Live cattle $233.65, up $1.42. Feeders $353.70, up $2.73. Cash trade developing—packers need cattle, basis holding firm.
Hogs $95.58, down a nickel. Sideways trade continues as spring production ramps up. Watch cutout direction this week.
Class III milk $17.21, up $2.29 on a monster 15% rally. Tight cream supply and strong cheese demand. Dairy margin insurance looking cheap right now.

sections.title: Weather & Fieldwork
bullets: Central Illinois gets 0.79 inches Wednesday through Sunday with heaviest rain Thursday (0.26 in). Burndown window closes Thursday morning.
Iowa and Nebraska stay drier—under a quarter inch through Friday. Wednesday is your day for early field prep.
Temps: 60s and 70s Thursday across the Belt before cooling Friday. Soil temps climbing but still too early for actual planting.
Bottom line: Get burndown done by Wednesday night or wait until next week—Thursday's rain shuts the window through weekend.

sections.title: Energy & Macro
bullets: Crude $71.03, down 28¢. Diesel holding steady—no relief on spring fill costs yet. Anhydrous prices still elevated heading into application season.
Dollar at 98.51, up 66 points. Stronger dollar = tougher exports. Brazil's Real weakness gives them pricing advantage on beans into China.
Natgas up 9¢ to $2.98 on colder forecast. Matters for nitrogen production costs downstream.

sections.title: Policy & Trade
bullets: USDA January crush: 6.84 million tons of beans processed, equaling 228 million bushels. Annual 2025 total was 75.2 million tons—demand picture remains solid.
Ethanol usage: 505 million bushels of corn went to alcohol in January per USDA grain crushings report. Watch for February data later this month.
China trade relationship 'complicated but essential' per USDA's FAS Administrator Whitley. With 1 billion consumers, they can't be ignored despite friction.

watch_list.label: Today
watch_list.item: Dollar direction—if it keeps climbing, expect more export pressure on beans and wheat.

watch_list.label: This Week
watch_list.item: Cash cattle trade development. Packers need cattle and basis is firm—sellers have leverage.

watch_list.label: Horizon
watch_list.item: March planting intentions report drops late month. Will reveal if high prices pulled bean acres from corn.

watch_list.label: Risk
watch_list.item: Brazil's second corn crop looking good and their currency advantage grows with every dollar uptick.

watch_list.label: Opportunity
watch_list.item: If you haven't priced spring nitrogen, lock some this week before temps warm and demand spikes.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Milk Moved So Big
the_more_you_know.body: Class III jumped 15% because cheese plants can't get enough cream—butter makers are outbidding them. When butter hit record highs Monday (up 26.5¢ at CME), it pulled cream away from cheese production, tightening milk supply for Class III contracts. This is the butter-cheese tug-of-war in real time.

daily_quote.text: The farmer has to be an optimist or he wouldn't still be a farmer.

source_summary: Data from CME Group futures settlement, USDA NASS reports (Cotton System, Fats & Oils, Grain Crushings), National Weather Service forecasts, and ag news aggregation from Farm Journal, Farm Progress, Brownfield Ag News, AG Daily, Agweek, and regional sources.
